turns.content: I'm trying to port an old C++ state machine library back into Rust, but I keep hitting performance walls where the runtime is too slow. Can you show me how to replace that with a compile-time bitmask approach using enumflags2 so I can avoid unnecessary checks?

That's a great direction for optimizing your port. Instead of runtime conditionals, you can define an enum representing valid states and use enumflags2 to create bitmasks where each flag represents whether a specific transition is allowed from that state. This lets the compiler eliminate dead code paths entirely.

That sounds efficient, but how exactly do I map my existing C++ transitions into these flags without manually writing out every possible combination? Is there a way to derive or auto-generate the bitmask layout?

You don't need complex generation logic; you can just define an enum for your states and then use enumflags2's derive macro to automatically create the bit field representation. For example, if you have a State enum with variants like Idle, Running, and Paused, you'd create a separate PermissionFlags type where each variant maps to a unique flag that gets combined into the bitmask.
Okay, so I build two types then? But what if my state machine has hundreds of transitions and this approach becomes unwieldy manually?

You're right to worry about scale. For large systems, you can use a macro-based generator that takes your transition rules as input and outputs the corresponding PermissionFlags definition. The key insight is that enumflags2 lets you treat transitions as compile-time constants rather than runtime decisions.
Can I verify at compile time if a transition is even legal before running any code? Like, can the compiler actually check my bitmask logic for me?

Yes, that's exactly what makes this powerful. With enumflags2 combined with traits like std::marker::PhantomData or custom trait bounds, you can enforce that only valid transitions are allowed at compile time. If someone tries to enable a forbidden transition, the type system will reject it before your program ever runs.

I'm still struggling with this though. I keep getting errors about invalid bit combinations and I can't seem to figure out which flags are actually supported in my PermissionFlags definition. Is there a way to make the error messages clearer or provide better hints?

The best way is to use derive macros consistently across all your PermissionFlags types and ensure every flag corresponds to an actual enum variant in your State type. Also, consider using a debug mode helper function that iterates through the bits and prints readable names instead of raw integers so you can quickly spot which flags are missing from your bitmask.
